findings_details.title: StrictMode test uses separate renderHook calls instead of StrictMode wrapper
findings_details.body: The test named 're-applies count after StrictMode cleanup cycle' (line 47-56) simulates the StrictMode cycle by calling renderHook twice sequentially rather than rendering inside React.StrictMode. This tests the end-state of sequential mount/unmount/mount but does not exercise the actual StrictMode double-invocation path (which fires synchronously within the same React reconciliation). For a hook as simple as useTabTitle this may be acceptable since the imperitive DOM write has no async component, but the test name overstates what is being verified. The test would pass even if the cleanup function had a bug that only manifested during StrictMode's synchronous teardown+reattach.

findings_details.title: Tab title count uses needsInputState.count but sidebar uses combined needsAttention — visible divergence
findings_details.body: useTabTitle(needsInputState.count) at line 248 counts only branches where needsInput is true (needs-input WebSocket events). The sidebar calls needsAttention (line 247, the combined check) which also includes sessions.needsAttention — branches with live terminal sessions that need attention for reasons beyond needs_input. If any branch triggers sessions.needsAttention without triggering needsInput, the sidebar shows it as needing attention but the tab title count is lower than the number of highlighted sidebar rows. The user sees 1 item highlighted in the sidebar and 'C3' in the tab title, which is contradictory.

findings_details.title: buildTabTitle renders fractional counts without integer guard
findings_details.body: The function signature accepts count: number. If called with a fractional value like 1.5, the guard (!Number.isFinite(count) || count <= 0) passes and the output is '1.5 items need attention — C3'. In practice count comes from [...map.values()].filter(Boolean).length which is always a non-negative integer, so this is a latent defect not currently reachable. However the interface does not enforce integer-only input and there is no test for fractional inputs. If a future caller passes a float (e.g. from averaging or rounding), the output is grammatically broken. Consider adding Math.floor/Math.round or constraining the type.

findings_details.title: Test plan items 7 and 8 assert no title flicker, but implementation always shows C3 briefly on load
findings_details.body:
Items 7 and 8 both assert the title shows the correct count "from first paint" with no flicker through "C3". This is factually incorrect. useNeedsInputState initialises with an empty Map (count=0), so useTabTitle(0) sets document.title to "C3" synchronously on first render. The /api/terminals fetch is async — count only increments after the fetch resolves (typically within ~1 second). There will always be a brief "C3" title before the count title appears.

The test plan checks as written will pass regardless of implementation quality, because the human reviewer cannot observe sub-100ms title changes without devtools open.

Fix: rewrite items 7 and 8 to describe observable reality — the title updates within ~1 second of first paint, not at first paint itself. The wording in items 2 and 4 ("within ~1 second") is the correct pattern to follow.

findings_details.title: buildTabTitle does not guard against NaN or non-finite count
findings_details.body: count is typed as number, which includes NaN and Infinity. NaN <= 0 is false, so NaN bypasses the guard on line 9 and reaches the noun branch, producing the string 'NaN items need attention — C3'. Infinity likewise produces 'Infinity items need attention — C3'. The caller is useNeedsInputState.count which derives from filter(Boolean).length — always a non-negative integer in practice — but the pure function buildTabTitle makes no contract restriction and its tests do not cover NaN or Infinity. Fix: add Number.isFinite(count) check alongside the <= 0 guard (e.g. if (!Number.isFinite(count) || count <= 0) return 'C3'), and add test cases for NaN and Infinity in tab-title.test.ts.

findings_details.title: useTabTitle cleanup unconditionally resets title — single-consumer assumption not documented in code
findings_details.body: The cleanup function on line 11-13 writes document.title = 'C3' unconditionally. If two components ever called useTabTitle simultaneously, the first to unmount would reset the title to 'C3' while the second is still mounted with a live count. Today only App.tsx calls this hook (verified by reading the diff), so there is no bug. However, the assumption is documented only in the review prompt, not in the source file. A future caller would not know they are violating a contract. Fix: add a JSDoc comment to useTabTitle stating the single-consumer assumption explicitly, e.g. '* NOTE: Must only be called from one mounted component at a time. The cleanup unconditionally restores the title to C3; concurrent callers would fight on unmount.'

findings_details.title: useTabTitle.test.ts has no StrictMode double-effect test
findings_details.body: useNeedsInputState.test.ts includes an explicit 'StrictMode safety' test for mount->unmount->remount (line 228). useTabTitle.test.ts covers unmount and remount as separate tests but not the sequential mount->cleanup->remount cycle that React StrictMode triggers in development. In that cycle, the cleanup fires ('C3') before the second mount's effect re-applies the count. The hook recovers correctly because the second mount re-runs buildTabTitle, but no test asserts the post-StrictMode title is correct rather than stuck at 'C3'. Fix: add a test that simulates the StrictMode double-invoke pattern: first unmount while count > 0 (verifies cleanup resets to C3), then immediately remount with the same count (verifies the count title is re-applied). This mirrors the existing remount test at line 39 but explicitly names it as StrictMode coverage.

findings_details.title: mountedRef never reset to true — silently drops initial seed in React StrictMode
findings_details.body: mountedRef is initialized to true (line 41) but the useEffect cleanup sets it to false (line 69) and never resets it. In React 18 StrictMode (dev), effects run twice: mount -> cleanup -> remount. After the first cleanup, mountedRef.current is false. The second mount's refresh() call completes the fetch, hits the guard at line 50 (if result.ok && mountedRef.current), and silently skips setMap. The initial seed from GET /api/terminals is dropped on the second StrictMode mount — the hook appears correctly seeded in prod but shows count=0 on first render in dev. Fix: add mountedRef.current = true; at the top of the useEffect body (line 59), before void refresh(), so each mount cycle rearms the guard. This pattern is established in react/issues — the ref should track the current mount's lifecycle, not the component instance lifecycle.

findings_details.title: count derivation not memoized — spread+filter on every render
findings_details.body: count: [...map.values()].filter(Boolean).length runs inline on every render (line 85). It allocates an array and iterates all entries even when the consumer only needs count and nothing else changed. For the typical map size (<100 branches) this is negligible, but it is unnecessarily eager. Wrapping in useMemo with [map] as the dependency would make the cost proportional to map changes rather than parent re-renders: const count = useMemo(() => [...map.values()].filter(Boolean).length, [map]). This is a nit — the current code is not wrong, just slightly wasteful.

findings_details.title: StrictMode test is mechanically identical to the prior remount test and does not exercise actual StrictMode double-invoke
findings_details.body: The test 're-applies count after StrictMode cleanup cycle' (line 47) performs mount → unmount → new renderHook, which is identical in structure to the test at line 39 ('re-applies the new count on remount after unmount'). The only difference is the count value stays the same (4→4) instead of changing (2→7). The comment explains React StrictMode fires effect → cleanup → effect within one mount cycle, but the test never uses a StrictMode wrapper, so that path is never executed. vitest+jsdom does not double-invoke effects. The test adds no coverage that line 39 does not already provide and its comment misleads future readers about what is actually verified.
